narrative: Dear [Student's Name], as a Black woman applying to law school, you've poured your heart into this journey, and I know receiving this rejection must feel incredibly disheartening amid the challenges many face in pursuing legal education. The admissions model we use looks at patterns from past students to predict the likelihood of passing the bar exam on the first try, and for you, it estimated a 51.6% chance of failure—meaning it saw your profile as slightly more likely to face that hurdle than not, which tipped the decision against admission. Your LSAT score of 36.50 was solid and close to the typical student's 36.70, standing out as the strongest positive factor by pulling the prediction toward expecting you to pass. However, your school decile rankings in Year 1 and Year 3—both at 2.00, well below the averages of about 5.71 and 5.53—emerged as the next most influential elements, unfortunately pushing the model toward foreseeing a higher risk of bar failure since they're lower than what succeeds for most students. Your undergraduate GPA of 2.30, compared to the average 3.21, and your upper-middle family income bracket also played smaller roles in softening the prediction slightly toward passing, much like your plan to attend full-time, which aligns with the vast majority of students. Overall, those early-year deciles carried the most weight in overriding your strengths, placing your academic trajectory below the typical successful applicant's path. This isn't a judgment on your potential or resilience—it's just how your specifics stacked up against historical patterns. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost that already competitive score, or focus on improving GPA through post-bac courses or targeted academic support to lift those decile rankings in future evaluations. Many students in similar situations, including Black women who've overcome early hurdles, go on to excel by addressing these key areas—reach out to advisors for personalized strategies, and know your determination can rewrite this story. You've got this; keep pushing.
